qualification_assessment.qualification_basis: agonia (G0074) names an intense inner struggle or anguish as its primary NT sense (Luke 22:44), and the Hebrew chul (H2342I) and the chi.lah/chil cluster (H2427B/A) designate writhing inner anguish experienced by the human person; both terms directly name internal non-physical states of extreme distress.
qualification_assessment.qualification_flag: Several terms in the cluster are predominantly somatic or external in their usage (gi.ach, odin, pig.gul-parallel ta.cha.lu.im which is excluded), and sha.vats has only one verse with uncertain meaning; the core qualification rests on agonia, chul, chi.lah/chil, odunaō, and basanizō.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Contest, violent struggle; agony, anguish, anxiety. The LSJ traces the term from athletic contest and gymnastic exercise to inner states of the mind — agony and anguish in contexts of fear and dread.
Pain, agony, sorrow, writhing, anguish; sub-definitions: writhing of fear, and anguish. Feminine form of chil (H2427A), derived from the chul root.
Pain, agony, sorrow, writhing, anguish; sub-definitions: writhing of fear, and anguish. Masculine form paired with chi.lah (H2427B). Source file notes the two as alternate forms of the same root.
Cramp, agony, anguish; meaning stated as uncertain in the sub-definition. Single occurrence in 2Sa 1:9.
To grieve, be anxious, be in agony; passive: to be in agony, be tormented, be distressed — either bodily or mentally. Active use: to cause pain or suffering.
Diseases. No sub-definitions given. Used of physical illness.
term_inventory.note: Source file marks this term 'Exclude word / This word only' — recorded for completeness per specification but excluded from inner-being analysis.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Birth pang, spasm of travail; plural: birth throes used metaphorically of catastrophic preliminary sufferings; from the Hebrew, extended to mean a stringent band or snare — Acts 2:24 uses it of the pangs of death (ōdines thanatou).

Pain, sorrow, travail, pang; sub-definitions: pains of travail and pains/pangs/sorrows. Related words indicate the same root covers cord, pledge, destruction, and piloting — suggesting semantic polysemy across distinct lexical fields.

To burst forth, draw forth, bring forth (Qal); to break forth (Hiphil). The Aramaic cognate means 'to strive'. Related words include proper nouns Gihon and Giah — the semantic connection to agony is through the bursting-forth imagery of birth or violent emergence.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: Verb Present Passive Participle Nominative Singular Neuter
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To torture, torment; passive: to be tortured, tormented, in pain. Etymologically from basanos (the touchstone for testing gold) — originally to apply the touchstone, then to examine or scrutinise, then to torture. NT uses: to afflict/torment (by disease, demonisation, waves), and passive: to be tormented or in pain.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: verb qal perfective first singular either gender
term_inventory.meaning_summary: To twist, whirl, dance, writhe, fear, tremble, travail, be in anguish, be pained. The root covers a remarkably wide semantic range — from physical dance and whirling to birth-travail writhing to inner anguish and fear. Hithpolel includes 'writhing, suffering torture' and 'waiting longingly'; Hithpalpel: to be distressed.

related_words.gloss: to twist: tremble / writh / give birth / dance / anticipate
to pledge / to destroy / be in labour

chil.reason: National-corporate register; pangs seize the inhabitants of Philistia as a collective response to hearing of God's acts — inner anguish present but applied to a population, not an individual.

ta.cha.lu.im.reason: Refers to physical diseases afflicting the land; external and somatic.
Physical disease causing death; somatic and external.
Divine healing of physical diseases; somatic referent.
Diseases of famine in the land; external somatic referent.

odin.reason: Eschatological-metaphorical register; birth pangs used as a figure for catastrophic preliminary sufferings before the end — inner-being dimension is indirect.
Parallel to Mat 24:8; same eschatological-metaphorical register.

odinēs thanatou ('pangs of death') applied to Christ's death and resurrection; the meaning note indicates a Hebrew loanshift from 'pang' to 'cord/snare' — whether the inner-being or the physical referent is primary cannot be determined from this verse and note alone.
Eschatological-metaphorical register; birth pains as a figure for sudden destruction — inner-being dimension present but indirect.

che.vel.reason: Describes the physical act of animals giving birth; no human inner-being referent.

Birth pangs applied to a son's spiritual failure to present himself at the critical moment — the physical birth imagery is explicitly metaphorical for a spiritual-inner crisis, but whether che.vel names the inner state or the external circumstance is ambiguous.

gi.ach.reason: Military manoeuvre (troops rushing out of ambush); no inner-being or agony referent.
Cosmic-creative register; the sea bursting from the womb — the birth-agony imagery is applied to creation, not to a human inner state.
Describes Behemoth's confidence when the river rushes against him; no agony referent.
Birth imagery applied to God's action in bringing the psalmist from the womb; the inner-being dimension is in the trust established at birth, not in agony per se.
Applied to Pharaoh as a dragon bursting forth in rivers; no agony or inner-being referent.

basanizō.reason: Applied to a boat beaten by waves; no human inner-being referent.
Applied to disciples making headway painfully against the wind — physical exertion, no inner agony as primary referent.

chul.reason: Describes Saul being badly wounded by archers; the English 'wounded' suggests a physical battle injury — the link to chul's writhing sense is not explicit in the translation.
Parallel to 1Sa 31:3; same physical battle-wound context.
Cosmic-nature register; the deep trembling/writhing at the presence of God — the chul imagery is applied to creation, not to human inner agony directly.
Personification of the sea speaking; the not-having-laboured or given birth is a negation of the birth-writhing image — indirect inner-being register.
National-collective register; Egypt in anguish over the report about Tyre — inner anguish is real but applied corporately.
Rhetorical question about birth; addresses the external circumstance of labour rather than the inner experience of agony.
Prophetic address to barren Zion; the not-having-been-in-labour is a negation of the birth-writhing image — extended register.
Describes miraculous birth before labour pains came — the absence of agony is the point; extended register.
National-birth imagery applied to Zion; corporate-eschatological register.
Land personified as trembling and writhing in pain under divine judgement; extended cosmic-land register.
Applied to a city (Pelusium) in great agony — personification of a city; extended register.
Peoples in anguish before an invading army — corporate register; inner anguish is real but applied collectively.
Cities writhing in anguish at regional news — corporate-city personification; extended register.

cross_registry_links.note: chal.cha.lah (anguish) appears as a related word under chi.lah, chil, and chul — all three share the chul root; chal.cha.lah was listed in the primary term list for 'abomination' as a cross-link and may correspond to a separate entry in the 181.
odunē is listed as a related word under odunaō and may be a separate entry in the 181 in its own right.
basanos (torment) appears in the primary term list for this word and is a related word under basanizō but has no independent term block; it may be a separate entry in the 181.
Dance terms share the chul root with the agony cluster — the same root covers writhing-in-pain and whirling-in-dance; relevant if dance is a separate entry in the 181.
The birth-pang metaphor is the dominant vehicle for agony language in the Hebrew term set; if labor/birth pangs is a separate entry in the 181 there will be substantial term overlap.

data_quality_flags.description: basanos appears in the primary term list (Matt 4:24, Luke 16:23) and in the STEP Bible Suggestion as a key term, and as a related word under basanizō, but has no independent term block in the STEP section of the source file; its Strong's number, grammatical form, meaning range, and verse list are absent.

The word analysis block for chi.lah shows a parsing token ('in/on/with (b)') preceding the term heading, indicating STEP captured an adjacent preposition as part of the anchor token; does not corrupt term data.
The word analysis block for sha.vats shows parsing tokens ('me (ni)', '[the] (h)') preceding the term heading; same STEP anchor-token display issue.
The word analysis block for ta.cha.lu.im shows a parsing token ('in/on/with (b)') preceding the term heading; same STEP anchor-token display issue.

sha.vats has only one verse and its meaning is explicitly stated as uncertain ('meaning uncertain') in the sub-definition; the analysis is necessarily restricted to the single occurrence and the bare meaning note.
chi.lah has 7 attested occurrences but only 1 verse is provided in the source file (Job 6:10); 6 occurrences are unaccounted for in the verse list.
gi.ach has 7 attested occurrences but only 5 verses are provided in the source file; 2 occurrences are absent and may contain relevant data.
The Strong's number in the source file is listed as G0928H rather than the standard G0928; the H suffix is not explained and may be a STEP sub-entry notation.

chil (H2427A) and chi.lah (H2427B) share the same occurrence count (7 x) and identical meaning sections, with the source file noting them as alternate forms; it is unclear whether the 7 occurrences are shared across both or separately attested for each.

The source file's primary term list includes basanos and chalchalah, but chalchalah does not appear in the STEP related word list as an independent entry — it appears only as a related word under chi.lah, chil, and chul. chalchalah has no term block in the file.
